elements.sent-2411-001: Most robotics applications are typically accompanied with safety restrictions that need to be satisfied with a high degree of confidence even in environments under uncertainty.
elements.sent-2411-002: Controlling the state distribution of a system and enforcing such specifications as distribution constraints is a promising approach for meeting such requirements.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this direction, covariance steering (CS) is an increasingly popular stochastic optimal control (SOC) framework for designing safe controllers via explicit constraints on the system covariance.
elements.sent-2411-004: Nevertheless, a major challenge in applying CS methods to systems with the nonlinear dynamics and chance constraints common in robotics is that the approximations needed are conservative and highly sensitive to the point of approximation.
elements.sent-2411-005: This can cause sequential convex programming methods to converge to poor local minima or incorrectly report problems as infeasible due to shifting constraints.
elements.sent-2411-006: This paper presents a novel algorithm for solving chance-constrained nonlinear CS problems that directly addresses this challenge.
elements.sent-2411-007: Specifically, we propose an operator splitting approach that temporarily separates the main problem into subproblems that can be solved in parallel.
elements.sent-2411-008: The benefit of this relaxation lies in the fact that it does not require all iterates to satisfy all constraints simultaneously prior to convergence, thus enhancing the exploration capabilities of the algorithm for finding better solutions.
elements.sent-2411-009: Simulation results verify the ability of the proposed method to find higher quality solutions under stricter safety constraints than standard methods on a variety of robotic systems.
elements.sent-2411-010: Finally, the applicability of the algorithm on real systems is confirmed through hardware demonstrations.
elements.sent-2411-011: Modern robots and autonomous systems typically include strict safety restrictions that are required to be met with a high degree of confidence.
elements.sent-2411-012: Indeed, in applications ranging from parking self-driving cars \cite{teng2023motion and landing autonomous aircrafts \cite{gautam2014survey to manipulators interacting with their environment \cite{alandoli2020critical , it is important to plan motions with safety guarantees under uncertainty.}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: For this reason, controlling distributions of robot trajectories via stochastic optimal control (SOC) is a promising field for meeting such performance specifications \cite{renardy2006introduction, bakolas2018finite, Okamoto19, balci2024density, wu2023general .}
elements.sent-2411-014: However, fully controlling the density of the state distribution, requires solving nonlinear PDEs, thus suffering from the curse of dimensionality \cite{renardy2006introduction .}
elements.sent-2411-015: This creates a requirement for developing methods that can effectively control the distributions of robotic systems while remaining scalable and computationally efficient.
elements.sent-2411-016: One emerging paradigm for synthesizing such controllers is covariance steering (CS) --- a SOC methodology that enforces constraints on the system state's covariance \cite{Hotz1987, chen2015optimal, bakolas2018finite, balci2024density, Okamoto19 .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Recent successful robotics applications of CS can be found in trajectory optimization \cite{Balci22, Yin22 , path planning \cite{Okamoto19, knaup2023safe , manipulation \cite{lee2022hierarchical and large-scale multi-agent control \cite{Saravanos21, Saravanos23 and others.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Since CS only concerns the first two moments of the system state, it is computationally tractable in comparison to density control problems.
elements.sent-2411-019: In addition, although the methodology originally focused on unconstrained linear systems, CS problems (CSPs) under nonlinear dynamics and chance constraints can be solved via iterative local approximations \cite{Ridderhof19, Yu2021, Saravanos22 .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{images/unicyle.png} \caption{The proposed algorithm steering a unicycle robot from an initial to a target distribution under probabilistic obstacle avoidance constraints. } \label{fig:unicycle}
elements.sent-2411-020: While CS is effective at designing controllers that explicitly reduce state uncertainty, solving chance-constrained nonlinear CSPs, remains difficult as conservative approximations are required in order to formulate tractable constraints.
elements.sent-2411-021: As a result, cluttered environments challenge existing approaches which might erroneously eliminate feasible minima, degrading performance or causing the solver to incorrectly report problems as infeasible.
elements.sent-2411-022: Such issues could even be compounded when iterative linearization methods are used that must repeatedly update their conservative approximations.
elements.sent-2411-023: This paper presents a novel CS algorithm for problems featuring nonlinear dynamics and state chance constraints, such as those imposed by workspace obstacles.
elements.sent-2411-024: The proposed method is derived based on an operator splitting formulation and solved using the alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM) \cite{Boyd11 .}
elements.sent-2411-025: Notably, the algorithm is tolerant to infeasible iterates before converging to a solution.
elements.sent-2411-026: This property allows the proposed algorithm to find solutions that feature superior costs under tighter constraints than traditional solvers on the same CSPs.
elements.sent-2411-027: These improvements are particularly important for robotics applications that involve obstacle rich environments and other performance requirements.
elements.sent-2411-028: The efficacy of the proposed algorithm is demonstrated in both extensive simulation testing and hardware examples.. Summary of Contributions.
elements.sent-2411-029: Our first main contribution is the formulation of an operator splitting algorithm for covariance steering problems involving nonlinear dynamics and state chance constraints.
elements.sent-2411-030: The method relies on an iterative approximation scheme for forming a sequence of convex optimization problems that are then decoupled into smaller subproblems which are solved in parallel with ADMM.
elements.sent-2411-031: This relaxation allows for intermediate iterates to be temporarily infeasible, thus enhancing the exploration capabilities of the algorithm in contrast with existing methods.
elements.sent-2411-032: Subsequently, we demonstrate in practice the ability of this approach to find superior solutions than traditional solvers through extensive simulations on linear, unicycle, and quadrotor systems.
elements.sent-2411-033: Finally, hardware experiments highlight the effectiveness of the proposed CS algorithm in controlling real systems.
elements.sent-2411-034: Controlling distributions of systems is a promising field for achieving safety requirements that are common in control and robotics \cite{renardy2006introduction, bakolas2018finite, Okamoto19, balci2024density, wu2023general, wu2023density .}
elements.sent-2411-035: In this direction, covariance steering has emerged as a tractable methodology for explicitly imposing constraints on the state mean and covariance of a system under uncertainty \cite{Hotz1987, chen2015optimal, bakolas2018finite, balci2024density, Okamoto19 .}
elements.sent-2411-036: This approach significantly differs from standard SOC approaches, e.g., linear-quadratic-Gaussian (LQG) control \cite{Stengel94 , that indirectly control the uncertainty through the cost function.}
elements.sent-2411-037: CS methods were initially formulated and studied under an infinite horizon setting during the 1980s \cite{Collins85, Hotz1987 .}
elements.sent-2411-038: At that time, their finite horizon counterparts were most likely ignored due to computational expensiveness as they result into semidefinite programs (SDPs).
elements.sent-2411-039: Recent advances in computational resources and optimization techniques, however, have made CS methods receive significant attention \cite{Yu2021, Knaup23, Ridderhof19, Okamoto19, Pilipovsky21, Balci24, Rapakoulias23, rapakoulias2024discrete, Balci22, Chen21, Chen20, Liu22, Yin22, Saravanos21, Saravanos22, Saravanos23 as a practical alternative to controlling the full density of the state distribution \cite{Chen21, Moyalan21 .}}
elements.sent-2411-040: Note that the latter approaches produce infinite-dimensional optimization problems which require the approximate solution of PDEs, thus reducing their scalability.
elements.sent-2411-041: In contrast, CS methods have been shown to provide solutions in reasonable computational time even for large-scale systems with hundreds to millions of states when combined with modern optimization techniques \cite{Saravanos21, Saravanos23 .}
elements.sent-2411-042: Robotics applications of CS include path planning \cite{Okamoto19, knaup2023safe , manipulation \cite{lee2022hierarchical , soft landing \cite{ridderhof2018uncertainty and multi-agent control \cite{Saravanos21, Saravanos22 to name only a few.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: Many recent methods have expanded CS problems to include nonlinear dynamics \cite{Ridderhof19, Yu2021, Saravanos22 and chance constraints \cite{Balci24, Pilipovsky21, Rapakoulias23, Okamoto19 through iterative approximation schemes.}}
elements.sent-2411-044: Such modifications are particularly relevant to robotics applications as they can capture extrinsic (e.g., obstacles avoidance) and intrinsic (e.g., joint limits) performance requirements placed on the robot.
elements.sent-2411-045: Nevertheless, the inclusion of nonlinear dynamics and chance constraints complicate CS problems significantly since they require various conservative approximations to render them convex.
elements.sent-2411-046: In fact, these approximations might limit the ability of such approaches to find high quality - or even feasible - solutions as the amount of constraints increases.
elements.sent-2411-047: Current literature avoids this problem by specializing to linear state constraints, while employing more expensive mixed-integer programming techniques for navigating through obstacles, e.g. \cite{Okamoto19 .}
elements.sent-2411-048: In contrast, this paper presents an algorithm for effectively controlling systems with nonlinear dynamics and obstacle constraints.
elements.sent-2411-049: Operator splitting approaches using the ADMM are becoming increasingly popular in control and robotics, thanks to their ability to decouple complex optimization problems into simpler subproblems and parallellize computations \cite{Sindhwani17, Saravanos23b .}
elements.sent-2411-050: Several successful applications can be found in trajectory optimization \cite{Sindhwani17, Wang21 , model predictive control \cite{rey2020admm , multi-agent control \cite{Saravanos23b, Pereira22, Chen2021b, Ferranti22, Schwager22 , manipulation \cite{Aydinoglu22 , legged locomotion \cite{Zhou20, Budhiraja19 , etc..}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-052: The expected value and covariance of $X$ are denoted $\func{E [X]$ and $\func{C [X]$ respectively.. If a r.v.}}
elements.sent-2411-053: The set $\set{S ^n_+$ ($\set{S ^n_{++}$) denotes the set of symmetric, positive semidefinite (definite) matrices with $n$ rows and columns.}}
elements.sent-2411-054: For two matrices $A, B$, the notation $A \succeq B$ indicates that $A - B \in \set{S ^n_+$.}
elements.sent-2411-055: For a function $f(x)$, its gradient at a point $\bar{x \in \set{R ^n$ is $\partial_x f(\bar{x )$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-056: Similarly, if $f(x)$ is real-valued, its Hessian at $\bar{x}$ is denoted $\partial_x^2 f(\bar{x )$.}
elements.sent-2411-057: All functions are assumed to be sufficiently smooth for the required derivatives to exist.
elements.sent-2411-058: Finally, we define the proximal operator ${\operatorname{prox _{\rho, f (z) = {\operatorname{argmin _x f(x) + \frac{\rho {2 \| x - z\|_2^2$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-059: Our method applies to discrete-time nonlinear dynamical systems with Gaussian process noise, i.e., \begin{equation}X_{t + 1} = f(X_t, u_t) + D(X_t) W_t, \label{eq:dynamics}\end{equation} where $X_t$ is the r.v.
elements.sent-2411-060: The initial and terminal conditions are given by: \begin{equation}\func{E}[X_0] = \mu_{ic}, \func{C}[X_0] = \Sigma_{ic}, \nonumber \func{E}[X_{t_f}] = \mu_{tc}, \func{C}[X_{t_f}] = \Sigma_{tc}. \label{eq:boundary conditions}\end{equation} The robot is subject to $N$ state constraints, i.e., workspace obstacles, defined by functions $h_i(x)$, $i = 1,\dots,N$.
elements.sent-2411-061: Each such function $h_i(x)$ defines a set $\set{O _i \define \{x\ |\ h_i(x) \leq 0\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-062: Since the support of $X_t$ is not compact, it is impossible to avoid the obstacles almost surely.
elements.sent-2411-063: This requirement is relaxed to only hold with high probability via the chance constraint: \begin{equation}\func{P}\{X_t \in \set{O}\} \leq \delta, \set{O} \define \bigcup_{i = 1}^N \set{O}_i. \label{eq:chance constraint}\end{equation} Finally, the controls are selected according to minimize in expectation a function $c(x)$ plus a quadratic control defined by $R \in \set{S ^n_{++}$ tallied at each time step $t$.}
elements.sent-2411-064: Therefore, the complete covariance steering problem is formulated as: \footnote{ The terminal covariance constraint can also be relaxed to an inequality constraint, i.e., $\func{C [X_{t_f ] \preceq \Sigma_{\mathrm{tc }$, which indicates that the terminal covariance of the robot simply needs to be ``smaller'' than $\Sigma_{\mathrm{tc }$.}}}}}
elements.section-2411-004: Chance Constrained Covariance Steering via Operator Splitting
elements.sent-2411-065: This section formulates a new algorithm for solving chance-constrained CSPs.
elements.sent-2411-067: First, the controller parameterization is described, which has a significant impact on the CSP.
elements.sent-2411-068: Next, a local convex approximation to \cref{eq:nonlinear cov steer prob is formulated and the operator splitting scheme is described.}
elements.sent-2411-069: Finally, the forward pass used to update the local approximation and complete algorithm are presented.
elements.sent-2411-070: An important aspect of solving \cref{eq:nonlinear cov steer prob is the parameterization of the $u_t$.}
elements.sent-2411-071: The choice of parameterization impacts the convex approximation of the chance constraint \cite{Balci24 , the number of decision variables, and how the feedback is performed.}
elements.sent-2411-072: As our method relies on iterative linear-quadratic approximations, we select a linear state feedback of the form: \begin{equation}u_t(x) = v_t + K_t (x - \mu_t). \label{eq:control law}\end{equation} The benefits of this formulation are that the number of decision variables is comparatively small, the feedback is determined directly by the deviation from the mean trajectory to help correct for nonlinearities during the forward pass, and projections onto the chance constraint set can be computed in parallel for each time step.
elements.sent-2411-073: The primary disadvantage is that the common approximation of the chance constraint as a second-order cone constraint is not possible, leading to a more conservative approximation.
elements.sent-2411-074: Other options present in the literature differ in how they use feedback to control the covariance \cite{Balci24 .}
elements.sent-2411-075: The choices include applying linear feedback to histories of states, histories of the disturbances \cite{Balci24 , or to the state of a zero-mean auxiliary stochastic process representing the impact of the noise on the system \cite{Ridderhof19, Pilipovsky21 .}}
elements.sent-2411-076: For most of these formulations, the chance constraint convex approximation is tighter, but lack the benefits of the selected parameterization.
elements.subsection-2411-004: Local Convex Approximation of the CS Problem
elements.sent-2411-077: This subsection performs a local approximation of \cref{eq:nonlinear cov steer prob using a nominal trajectory $\tau = (\bar{x _{0:t_f , \bar{u _{0:t_f , \bar{\Sigma _{0:t_f )$ satisfying $\bar{x _{t + 1 = f(\bar{x _t, \bar{u _t)$.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: In \cref{ssec:trajectory update , how this trajectory is updated from an initial guess is discussed.. Dynamics.}
elements.sent-2411-079: In order to produce local solutions to the covariance steering problem \cref{eq:nonlinear cov steer prob , a first-order Taylor expansion is performed, yielding affine dynamics, \begin{equation}X_{t + 1} = A_t X_t + B_t u_t + D_t w_t + d_t,\ w_t \sim \dist{N}(0, I),\end{equation} where $A_t \define \partial_x f(\bar{x _t, \bar{u _t), B_t \define \partial_u f(\bar{x _t, \bar{u _t), D_t \define D(\bar{x _t),$ and $d_t \define f(\bar{x _t, \bar{u _t) - A_t \bar{x _t - B_t \bar{u _t$.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}X_{t + 1} = A_t X_t + B_t u_t + D_t w_t + d_t,\ w_t \sim \dist{N}(0, I),\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-080: Under the feedback law \cref{eq:control law , the state distribution remains Gaussian and the mean and covariance evolve as: \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\mu_{t + 1} = A_t \mu_t + B_t v_t + d_t, \label{eq:mean dynamics} \Sigma_{t + 1} = (A_t + B_t K_t) \Sigma_t \transp{(A_t + B_t K_t)} + D_t \transp{D}_t,\label{eq:cov dynamics}\end{equation} \end{subequations Define the dynamically feasible sets of $\mu_{0:t_f}$ and $\Sigma_{0:t_f}$ as: \begin{equation}\set{F}_\mu \define \qty{\mu_0, \dots, \mu_{t_f} \in \set{R}^n\ \middle|\ (\mu_0, \mu_{t_f}) = (\mu_{ic}, \mu_{tc}), \text{\cref{eq:mean dynamics}}},\nonumber \set{F}_\Sigma \define \qty{\Sigma_0, \dots, \Sigma_{t_f} \in \set{S}_+^n\ \middle| (\Sigma_0, \Sigma_{t_f}) = (\Sigma_{ic}, \Sigma_{tc}), \text{\cref{eq:cov dynamics}}}.\nonumber\end{equation} Cost Function.}}}
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}\mu_{t + 1} = A_t \mu_t + B_t v_t + d_t, \label{eq:mean dynamics} \Sigma_{t + 1} = (A_t + B_t K_t) \Sigma_t \transp{(A_t + B_t K_t)} + D_t \transp{D}_t,\label{eq:cov dynamics}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-081: Similarly, the cost function is approximated to second-order.
elements.sent-2411-082: Up to a constant, it is given by: \begin{equation}\bar{c}_t(x, u) \define \frac{1}{2}(\transp{x} Q_t x + \transp{u} R u) + \transp{q}_t x.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation}\bar{c}_t(x, u) \define \frac{1}{2}(\transp{x} Q_t x + \transp{u} R u) + \transp{q}_t x.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-083: The expected cost also factors into a mean component and steering component.
elements.sent-2411-084: Leading to two separate objectives: \begin{equation}J_{\mu}(\mu_{0:t_f}, v_{0:t_f}) \define \sum_{t = 0}^{t_f - 1} \bar{c}_{t}(\mu_t, v_t), J_{\Sigma}(\Sigma_{0:t_f}, K_{0:t_f}) \define \sum_{t = 0}^{t_f - 1} \trace(Q_t \Sigma_t) + \trace(R_t K_t \Sigma_t \transp{K}_t).\nonumber\end{equation} Chance Constraint.
elements.sent-2411-085: The chance constraint \cref{eq:chance constraint is, in general, intractable since it requires evaluating a multidimensional integral.}
elements.sent-2411-086: We follow a standard approach based on Boole's inequality to produce a tractable conservative approximation.
elements.sent-2411-087: Specifically, for a state $X_t$, it holds that: \begin{equation}\func{P}\qty{X_t \in \set{O}} \leq \sum_{i = 1}^N \func{P}\qty{h_i(X_t) \leq 0}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-088: Therefore, the following constraint satisfies \cref{eq:chance constraint , \begin{equation}\func{P}\qty{h_i(X_t) \leq 0} \leq \delta/N, i = 1, \dots, N.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-089: Next, since $h_i(x)$ is, in general, nonlinear, an approximation is required to formulate a tractable constraint.
elements.sent-2411-090: Specifically, each obstacle is formulated as a half-plane constraint given by the linearization of $h_i(x)$, i.e., \begin{equation}\bar{h}_{i, t}(x) \define \transp{a}_{i, t} x + b_{i, t} \leq 0,\end{equation} where $a_{i, t \define \partial_x h_i(\bar{x _{t ), b_{i, t \define h(\bar{x _t) - \transp{a _{i, t}\bar{x _t$ Since $X_t$ is Gaussian, each hyperplane constraint can be formulated as a deterministic function of $(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$, \begin{equation}\func{P}\qty{\transp{a}_{i, t} X_t + b_{i, t} \leq 0} \leq \delta' \iff g_{i, t}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t) \leq 0,\end{equation} where: \begin{equation}g_{i, t}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t) \define \transp{a}_{i, t} \mu_t + b_{i, t} + \inv{\Phi}\qty(1 - \delta')\sqrt{\transp{a}_{i, t}\Sigma_t a_{i, t}} \leq 0.\nonumber\end{equation} Here, $\inv{\Phi (\cdot)$ is the inverse of the Gaussian cumulative density function (CDF) and $\delta' \define \nicefrac{\delta {N}$.}}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-013: \begin{equation}\func{P}\qty{\transp{a}_{i, t} X_t + b_{i, t} \leq 0} \leq \delta' \iff g_{i, t}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t) \leq 0,\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-014: \begin{equation}g_{i, t}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t) \define \transp{a}_{i, t} \mu_t + b_{i, t} + \inv{\Phi}\qty(1 - \delta')\sqrt{\transp{a}_{i, t}\Sigma_t a_{i, t}} \leq 0.\nonumber\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-091: In the desired case, $\delta' < 0.5$, the function $g_i(\mu, \Sigma)$ is concave in $\Sigma$, so the constraint is unfortunately still nonconvex.
elements.sent-2411-092: However, since it is a concave function a linearization of $g_i(\mu, \Sigma)$ will provide a conservative approximation.
elements.sent-2411-093: Specifically, $g_i(\mu, \Sigma)$ is approximated by a first-order Taylor expansion, \begin{equation}\bar{g}_{i, t}(\mu, \Sigma) \define c_{i, t} + \transp{w}_{i, t} \mu + \trace\qty(G_{i, t}\transp{\Sigma}),\nonumber\end{equation} where $w_{i, t \define \partial_\mu g_{i, t (\bar{x _t, \bar{\Sigma _t), G_{i, t \define \partial_\Sigma g_{i, t (\bar{x _t, \bar{\Sigma _t),$ and $c_{i, t = g_{i, t (\bar{\mu _t, \bar{\Sigma _t) - \transp{w _{i, t \bar{x - \trace(\smash{G_{i, t \transp{\bar{\Sigma )$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-094: Denote the set of means and covariance matrices satisfying the approximated constraint by: $\set{G _{t \define \{(\mu, \Sigma)\ |\ \bar{g _{t (\mu, \Sigma) \leq 0,\ i = 1, \dots, N \}$.. Proximal Regularization.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-095: Since the covariance steering problem being formed uses local approximations to the nonlinear dynamics and costs, it is necessary to control how far the solution deviates from the trajectory used by linearization.
elements.sent-2411-096: In this paper, a proximal method is used.
elements.sent-2411-098: The parameters $\alpha_\mu, \alpha_\Sigma$ control the strength of the regularization.
elements.sent-2411-100: Following the preceding developments, we can formulate a local, convex approximation to \cref{eq:nonlinear cov steer prob given a trajectory $(\bar{x _{0:t_f , \bar{u _{0:t_f , \bar{\Sigma _{0:t_f )$: \begin{mini |s| { {J_{\mu + J_{\Sigma + J_{\mathrm{px , { { \addConstraint{(\mu_t, \Sigma_t) \in \set{G _t, {\ \mu_{0:t_f \in \set{F _\mu, {\ \Sigma_{0:t_f \in \set{F _\Sigma .. \end{mini.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-101: In the absence of chance constraints, problem \cref{eq:lq cov steer prob can be decoupled into a mean subproblem using the open-loop controls $v_{0:t_f-1}$ and a covariance one which uses the feedback matrices $K_{0:t_f-1}$.}
elements.sent-2411-102: These two problems can then be solved in parallel.
elements.sent-2411-103: Nevertheless, even a single obstacle avoidance chance constraint produces a coupling resulting in a sizeable SDP that cannot be parallelized \cite{Balci24 .}
elements.sent-2411-104: In addition, using such an approach within an iterative linearization scheme would require feasible initializations and intermediate solutions during all iterations, which might significantly reduce the exploration capabilities of the algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-105: To address these issues, we propose an operator splitting scheme using the ADMM to decouple chance constraint satisfaction from the two steering problems.
elements.sent-2411-106: This reformulation is performed by first introducing copies of the mean and covariance decision variables.
elements.sent-2411-107: The copy $(\mu^{\mathrm{s _{0:t_f , \Sigma^{\mathrm{s _{0:t_f )$ is used for the steering problems, the copy $(\mu^{\mathrm{cc _{0:t_f , \Sigma^{\mathrm{cc _{0:t_f )$ is used to enforce the chance constraints, and the copy $(\mu^{\mathrm{cn _{0:t_f , \Sigma^{\mathrm{cn _{0:t_f )$ is used to ensure consensus.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-108: For notational convenience, we also define the following variable groups: \begin{equation}z_\mu \define (v_{0:t_f}, \mu^{s}_{0:t_f}), z_\Sigma \define (K_{0:t_f}, \Sigma^{s}_{0:t_f}), z_{cc} \define (\mu^{cc}_{0:t_f}, \Sigma^{cc}_{0:t_f}), z_{cn} \define (\mu^{cn}_{0:t_f}, \Sigma^{cn}_{0:t_f}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-109: Next, we form three objectives that include the constraints via indicator functions: \begin{equation}\tilde{J}_{\mu}(z_\mu) \define J_{\mu}(\mu_{0:t_f}, v_{0:t_f}) + I_{\set{F}_\mu}(\mu_{0:t_f}), \tilde{J}_{\Sigma}(z_\Sigma) \define J_{\Sigma}(\Sigma_{0:t_f}, K_{0:t_f}) + I_{\set{F}_\Sigma}(\Sigma_{0:t_f}),\nonumber \tilde{J}_{cc}(z_{cc}) \define I_{\set{G}_1}(\mu^{cc}_{1}, \Sigma^{cc}_{1}) + \dots + I_{\set{G}_{t_f}}(\mu^{cc}_{t_f - 1}, \Sigma^{cc}_{t_f - 1}).\nonumber\end{equation} Then, we can reformulate problem \cref{eq:lq cov steer prob as \begin{mini |s| { {\tilde{J _{\mu (z_\mu) + \tilde{J _{\Sigma (z_\Sigma) + \tilde{J _{\mathrm{cc (z_{\mathrm{cc ) + J_{\mathrm{px (z_{\mathrm{cn ), { { \addConstraint{\mu^{\mathrm{s _{0:t_f = \mu^{\mathrm{cn _{0:t_f , {\quad \Sigma^{\mathrm{s _{0:t_f = \Sigma^{\mathrm{cn _{0:t_f { \addConstraint{\mu^{\mathrm{cc _{0:t_f = \mu^{\mathrm{cn _{0:t_f , {\quad \Sigma^{\mathrm{cc _{0:t_f = \Sigma^{\mathrm{cn _{0:t_f { .}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-018: \begin{equation}\tilde{J}_{\mu}(z_\mu) \define J_{\mu}(\mu_{0:t_f}, v_{0:t_f}) + I_{\set{F}_\mu}(\mu_{0:t_f}), \tilde{J}_{\Sigma}(z_\Sigma) \define J_{\Sigma}(\Sigma_{0:t_f}, K_{0:t_f}) + I_{\set{F}_\Sigma}(\Sigma_{0:t_f}),\nonumber \tilde{J}_{cc}(z_{cc}) \define I_{\set{G}_1}(\mu^{cc}_{1}, \Sigma^{cc}_{1}) + \dots + I_{\set{G}_{t_f}}(\mu^{cc}_{t_f - 1}, \Sigma^{cc}_{t_f - 1}).\nonumber\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-110: The (scaled) augmented Largrangian (AL) of \cref{eq:admm cov steer prob is given by \begin{equation}L_\rho = \tilde{J}_{\mu}(z_\mu) + \tilde{J}_{\Sigma}(z_\Sigma) + \tilde{J}_{cc}(z_{cc}) + J_{px}(z_{cn}) \nonumber + \frac{\rho}{2} \Big[ \left\| \mu^{s} - \mu^{cn} + \lambda_1 \right\|_2^2 + \left\| \mu^{cc} - \mu^{cn} + \lambda_2 \right\|_2^2 + \left\| \Sigma^{s} - \Sigma^{cn} + \Lambda_1 \right\|_F^2 + \left\| \Sigma^{cc} - \Sigma^{cn} + \Lambda_2 \right\|_F^2 \Big], \nonumber\end{equation} where $\rho$ is a consensus penalty parameter and $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \Lambda_1, \Lambda_2$ are the dual variables for the corresponding equality constraints.}
elements.sent-2411-111: In the previous expression, the time indices have been temporarily dropped for notational brevity.

elements.sent-2411-114: The second ADMM block involves minimizing the AL w.r.t.
elements.sent-2411-115: Thus, iterates of the resulting ADMM algorithm are allowed to be temporarily infeasible.
elements.sent-2411-116: In practice, this allows the proposed algorithm to find feasible solutions to \cref{eq:lq cov steer prob when common SDP solvers might fail.}
elements.sent-2411-117: A procedure (i.e., ForwardPass in \cref{alg:csos ) to update $\tau$ once the ADMM converges is needed to complete the algorithm.}
elements.sent-2411-118: This update can be achieved using various methods to approximately compute the mean and covariance corresponding to the nonlinear dynamics \cref{eq:dynamics .}
elements.sent-2411-119: One possible option available is to estimate these quantities from many samples of \cref{eq:dynamics using the most recently computed controller parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-120: While sampling can be performed in parallel, larger state spaces and longer time horizons can still require a prohibitive number of samples.
elements.sent-2411-121: In such cases, if the process noise is small and the regularizer is well-tuned, $\bar{x _{0:t_f , \bar{u _{0:t_f}$ can be estimated by simply propagating the mean dynamics (i.e., \cref{eq:dynamics with the process noise term removed) using the new control parameters and $\Sigma_{0:t_f ^\mathrm{s}$ as an approximation for $\bar{\Sigma _{0:t_f}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-122: The latter method was found to work quite well and is the method used in \cref{sec:simulations and \cref{sec:hardware .}}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Operator Splitting Scheme} \label{alg:os} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require Initial $z_\mu, z_\Sigma, z_{\mathrm{cc}}, z_{\mathrm{cn}}$, $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \Lambda_1, \Lambda_2$, $\rho$. \Repeat \State $z_\mu, z_\Sigma, z_{\mathrm{cc}} \gets$ Solve \cref{eq: admm block 1}. \quad \quad \quad \# In parallel \State $z_{\mathrm{cn}} \gets$ Update with \cref{eq: admm block 2}. \quad \quad \quad \# In parallel \State $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \Lambda_1, \Lambda_2 \gets$ Update with \cref{eq: admm block dual}. \Until{Converged} \end{algorithmic}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Covariance Steering via Operator Splitting} \label{alg:csos} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require Initial trajectory $\tau^{(0)} \define (\bar{x}^{(0)}_{0:t_f}, \bar{u}_{0:t_f}^{(0)}, \bar{\Sigma}_{0:t_f}^{(0)})$. \Repeat \State $A_{0:t_f}^{(k)}, B_{0:t_f}^{(k)}, d_{0:t_f}^{(k)}, Q_{0:t_f}^{(k)}, q_{0:t_f}^{(k)}, c_{i, 0:t_f}^{(k)}, w_{i, 0:t_f}^{(k)}, G_{i, 0:t_f}^{(k)}$ $\gets$ TaylorExpansions$(\tau^{(k)})$. \State $\mu_{0:t_f}^{(k)}, \Sigma_{0:t_f}^{(k)}, v_{0:t_f}^{(k)}, K_{0:t_f}^{(k)} \gets$ Solve with Alg. \ref{alg:os}. \State $\tau^{(k + 1)} \gets$ ForwardPass$(\mu_{0:t_f}^{(k)}, \Sigma_{0:t_f}^{(k)}, v_{0:t_f}^{(k)}, K_{0:t_f}^{(k)})$. \Until{Converged} \end{algorithmic}
elements.figure-2411-002: \subfloat[]{ \includegraphics[width=0.65\columnwidth, trim={0cm 0cm 0cm 0cm},clip]{images/linear_sdp2.png} \label{fig:linear_sdp} } \subfloat[]{ \includegraphics[width=0.65\columnwidth, trim={0cm 0cm 0cm 0cm},clip]{images/linear_admm2.png} \label{fig:linear_admm} } \subfloat[]{ \includegraphics[width=0.635\columnwidth, trim={0cm 0cm 0cm 0cm},clip]{images/infeasibility_sdp.png} \label{fig: issue} } \caption{The results of the ablation study conducted in \cref{ssec:ablation}. Our algorithm found a solution that exhibits a lower cost while also satisfying tighter safety constraints. a) The solution found by the baseline method. Notice that, despite a loser safety requirement, the covariances are much tighter and the trajectory takes larger steps compared to our solution. This is an artifact of the optimizer stuck in a bad local minima where the only feasible solution requires precisely placed means and covariances. b) The solution found by our algorithm. c) This schematic visually depicts why traditional solvers struggle to to solve \cref{eq:lq cov steer prob} as part of iterative algorithms. The trajectory $\tau_{\mathrm{orig}}$ is the trajectory around about which the approximations occur. The half-planes shown correspond to the linearized chance constraints. The set of points that are feasible with respect to the chance constraint and those that are dynamically feasible cannot both be satisfied. This failure mode can occur even in the simple case of a double integrator when the trajectory is feasible.} \label{fig:csetup}
elements.sent-2411-123: This section demonstrates the effectiveness of our method on controlling robotic systems.
elements.sent-2411-124: We start with illustrating a task of safely navigating a unicycle while avoiding multiple obstacles (\cref{sec: sim results - unicycle ).}
elements.sent-2411-125: Then, a quadrotor task that features significantly more challenging dynamics along with an obstacle is addressed (\cref{sec: sim results - quadrotor ).}
elements.sent-2411-126: Finally, an ablation study is performed in \cref{ssec:ablation that compares the operator splitting method with solving \cref{eq:lq cov steer prob as a single SDP.}}
elements.sent-2411-127: All optimization problems are solved with CVXPY \cite{diamond2016cvxpy and MOSEK on Intel i9-14900K.. \cite{mosek_python .}}
elements.sent-2411-128: We first consider a unicycle robot with state $x = (\mathrm{x , \mathrm{y , \theta)$ and control input $u = (v, \omega)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-129: The time step is $\Delta t = 0.1$ and $t_f = 50$.
elements.sent-2411-130: The initial and target covariances are specified through $\Sigma_0=0.2I_3$ and $\Sigma_{t_f \preceq 0.2I_3$, while $D_t=0.01I_3$.}
elements.sent-2411-131: We have chosen a quadratic cost function with $Q=0.001I_3$ and $R=0.1I_2$.
elements.sent-2411-132: The solution was obtained by running Algorithm \ref{alg:csos for 10 iterations and solving \cref{eq:lq cov steer prob using 15 iterations of ADMM.}}
elements.sent-2411-133: For this task, our method required 2.13 seconds for computations.
elements.sent-2411-134: Table \ref{tabl:results compares the approximate objective value from the last solution of \cref{eq:lq cov steer prob with the mean cost from 200 sample trajectories.}}
elements.sent-2411-135: The solution and sample trajectories of the unicycle are depicted in \cref{fig:unicycle .}
elements.figure-2411-003: [] \begin{tabular}{@{}lcccc@{}} Experiment Cost Safety Prob. Unicycle Optimizer 15.96 0.990 Estimated 15.96 0.995 [0.05em] Quadrotor Optimizer 762.00 0.990 Estimated 821.38 1.000 [0.05em] Robotorium Optimizer 7.94 0.900 Estimated 8.06 0.970 \end{tabular} \caption{Summary of Experimental Results} \label{tabl:results}

elements.sent-2411-138: Estimated costs from the value of the last approximation and sample trajectories are available in \cref{tabl:results .}
elements.sent-2411-139: The solution was computed in 25.68 seconds.
elements.sent-2411-140: To avoid numerical issues resulting from large angular values, the initial state is sampled from a truncated Gaussian.
elements.sent-2411-141: Our method successfully satisfies the required probability of safety while reaching the goal location.

elements.sent-2411-142: To analyze the performance capabilities of our method, it is important to compare our method with a baseline where \cref{eq:lq cov steer prob is treated as a single SDP without operator splitting.}
elements.sent-2411-143: To simplify the comparison, we use a double integrator with $\Delta t = 0.2$, $\Sigma_0 = 0.2I_4$, $\Sigma_{t_f =0.05I_4$ and $D_t = I_4$.}
elements.sent-2411-144: The cost function is quadratic with $Q=0.01I_4$ and $R=0.005I_2$.
elements.sent-2411-145: Both methods were run for 10 iterations with warm starting.
elements.sent-2411-146: In our algorithm, ADMM was run for 15 iterations.
elements.sent-2411-147: Table \ref{tabl:sdp_admm_results summarizes the results from our experiment.}
elements.sent-2411-148: The baseline tends to converge more quickly but fails to generate a feasible solution when the required probability of safety is $\delta' = 0.99$ even if the dynamics are linear.
elements.sent-2411-149: In particular, it is only able to find a feasible solution when $\delta' = 0.9$.
elements.sent-2411-150: On the contrary, our method found a solution with the tighter constraint $\delta' = 0.99$ that also features a lower cost.
elements.sent-2411-151: Figures \ref{fig:linear_sdp and \ref{fig:linear_admm show the resulting trajectories from the two methods.}}
elements.sent-2411-152: Since our method allows the iterates to temporarily relax the constraints, it can find feasible trajectories in cases where the baseline method fails since the latter always requires all constraints to be satisfied simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-153: This poses a problem when constructing the convex approximation \cref{eq:lq cov steer prob about a new trajectory.}
elements.sent-2411-154: Essentially, when linearizing the obstacle constraints about a new trajectory, the intersection of the set of dynamically feasible points and points that satisfy the chance constraints becomes disjoint.
elements.sent-2411-155: Figure \ref{fig: issue highlights an occurrence of this limitation.}
elements.sent-2411-156: On the other hand, our method circumvents this issue since the dynamical and chance constraints are not required to be simultaneously satisfied prior to convergence.

elements.sent-2411-157: To show the usefulness of the controllers synthesized by our algorithm, we conduct a hardware experiment on the Robotarium platform \cite{Pickem17 , using a unicycle robot with the same unicycle dynamics as in Section \ref{sec: sim results - unicycle .}}

elements.sent-2411-159: We have set $\Delta t = 0.2$, $t_f = 200$, an initial covariance $\Sigma_0 = 0.05I_3$, terminal covariance $\Sigma_{t_f =0.05I_3$ and a position noise $D_t = 0.01I_3$.}
elements.sent-2411-160: For simplicity we have again chosen a quadratic cost with $Q=0.01I_3$ and $R=0.1I_2$.
elements.sent-2411-161: In addition to the obstacle constraints, we enforce a conservative wheel velocity constraints, $-b_{max \leq G u_t \leq b_{max}$ while solving the mean steering problem in \cref{eq: admm block 1 , where $G=(R/2) [2,L; 2,L]$, $b_{max =[7;7] \ \mathrm{rad /\mathrm{s ^2$, wheel radius $R = 0.016\mathrm{m}$ and axle length $L=0.11 \mathrm{m}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-162: The solution was obtained by running 20 iterations of Algorithm \ref{alg:csos and using 10 ADMM iterations to solve \cref{eq:lq cov steer prob .}}
elements.sent-2411-163: Our algorithm took 19.03 seconds to run.
elements.sent-2411-164: The Robotarium platform was used to perform 100 trials with different initial conditions sampled and the results are shown in \cref{tabl:results .}
elements.sent-2411-165: As shown, the proposed controller exhibits reliable performance on a real robotic system.
elements.sent-2411-166: During the 100 runs, only 3 runs violated the safety constraint.
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=0.9\columnwidth]{images/robotarium_new.png} \caption{Example run of Robotarium GritsBot navigating around virtual obstacles. } \label{fig:robotarium}
elements.sent-2411-167: This article demonstrated that the proposed operator splitting method for solving nonlinear CSPs is effective at finding solutions with better performance and safety than extant methods.
elements.sent-2411-168: This improvement is due to the fact that the algorithm is tolerant to infeasible iterates during the optimization feature that often appears due to the sensitivity and conservatism of the necessary approximations.
elements.sent-2411-169: The advantages of the method were demonstrated on a variety of tasks including both simulation and hardware experiments.
elements.sent-2411-170: There is a variety of possible extensions and applications that can be built on top of the presented method.
elements.sent-2411-171: One direction is to switch the ADMM method used with a variant that more naturally handles probability distributions, e.g., Bregman ADMM \cite{Wang14 .}
elements.sent-2411-172: Another direction could be combining the algorithm with sampling-based motion planning \cite{Lavalle06 for finding safe plans, similar in spirit to LQR-Trees \cite{Tedrake10 .}}
elements.sent-2411-173: This paper presents a novel algorithm for solving distribution steering problems featuring nonlinear dynamics and chance constraints.
elements.sent-2411-174: Covariance steering (CS) is an emerging methodology in stochastic optimal control that poses constraints on the first two moments of the state distribution --- thereby being more tractable than full distributional control.
elements.sent-2411-175: Nevertheless, a significant limitation of current approaches for solving nonlinear CS problems, such as sequential convex programming (SCP), is that they often generate infeasible or poor results due to the large number of constraints.
elements.sent-2411-176: In this paper, we address these challenges, by proposing an operator splitting CS approach that temporarily decouples the full problem into subproblems that can be solved in parallel.
elements.sent-2411-177: This relaxation does not require intermediate iterates to satisfy all constraints simultaneously prior to convergence, which enhances exploration and improves feasibility in such non-convex settings.
elements.sent-2411-178: Simulation results across a variety of robotics applications verify the ability of the proposed method to find better solutions even under stricter safety constraints than standard SCP.
elements.sent-2411-179: Finally, the applicability of our framework on real systems is also confirmed through hardware demonstrations (Video: \url{https://youtu.be/UCyYcDITO2Q ).}
elements.sent-2411-180: In modern control applications, autonomous systems must satisfy a number of specified design and performance criteria with a high degree of confidence to ensure their reliable performance.
elements.sent-2411-181: For this reason, controlling distributions of system trajectories via stochastic optimal control (SOC) is a promising field for meeting such performance specifications \cite{Renardy06, Bakolas18, Okamoto19, Balci24, Wu23a .}
elements.sent-2411-182: Nevertheless, fully controlling the density of the state distribution is an infinite-dimensional problem which suffers from the curse of dimensionality \cite{Renardy06 .}
elements.sent-2411-183: As such, developing methods that can effectively control the distributions of nontrivial systems while remaining scalable and computationally efficient remains a significant challenge for safe autonomy.
elements.sent-2411-184: One emerging paradigm for synthesizing such controllers is covariance steering (CS) --- a SOC methodology that enforces constraints on the mean and covariance of the state \cite{Hotz1987, Chen15, Bakolas18, Balci24, Okamoto19 .}
elements.sent-2411-185: Successful applications of CS can be found in trajectory optimization \cite{Balci22, Yin22 , path planning \cite{Okamoto19, Knaup23a , manipulation \cite{Lee22 , power systems \cite{Khalil25 , and large-scale multi-agent control \cite{Saravanos21, Saravanos23 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-186: Although CS originally focused on unconstrained linear systems, CS problems (CSPs) under nonlinear dynamics and chance constraints can be solved via iterative local approximations using algorithms like sequential convex programming (SCP) \cite{Ridderhof19, Yu2021, Saravanos22 .}
elements.sent-2411-187: While CS is effective at designing controllers that explicitly model uncertainty, solving chance-constrained, nonlinear CSPs remains difficult.
elements.sent-2411-188: The approximations required to formulate tractable constraints at each step of iterative algorithms tend to be very conservative.
elements.sent-2411-189: As a result, heavily constrained problems (e.g., motion planning with many obstacles) challenge existing SCP methods that may erroneously eliminate feasible minima, degrading performance or causing the solver to incorrectly report problems as infeasible.
elements.sent-2411-190: Such issues further compound over iterations of SCP algorithms as they require repeated conservative approximations.
elements.sent-2411-191: These improvements are particularly important for applications that require trajectories to satisfy many state space constraints along with other performance requirements.
elements.sent-2411-192: The efficacy of the proposed algorithm is shown via extensive simulation of both linear and nonlinear motion planning problems with nonconvex state spaces as well as hardware experiments.. Summary of Contributions.
elements.sent-2411-193: Subsequently, we demonstrate in practice the ability of this approach to find solutions superior to those produced by traditional solvers in extensive simulations of various linear and nonlinear motion planning problems.
elements.sent-2411-194: Controlling distributions of systems is a promising field for achieving safety requirements that are common in control and robotics \cite{Renardy06, Bakolas18, Okamoto19, Balci24, Wu23a, Wu23b .}
elements.sent-2411-195: In this direction, covariance steering has emerged as a tractable methodology for explicitly imposing constraints on the mean and covariance of the system state under stochastic uncertainty \cite{Hotz1987, Chen15, Bakolas18, Balci24, Okamoto19 .}
elements.sent-2411-196: This approach significantly differs from standard SOC approaches, e.g., linear-quadratic-Gaussian (LQG) control \cite{Stengel94 , which indirectly control the uncertainty through the cost function.}
elements.sent-2411-197: At that time, their finite-horizon counterparts were most likely ignored due to the necessity of solving associated semidefinite programs (SDP), which was computationally expensive.
elements.sent-2411-198: Advances in computational resources and optimization techniques, however, have led to finite-horizon CS methods receiving significantly renewed interest \cite{Yu2021, Knaup23b, Ridderhof19, Okamoto19, Pilipovsky21, Balci24, Rapakoulias23, Rapakoulias24, Balci22, Chen21, Chen20, Liu22, Yin22, Saravanos21, Saravanos22, Saravanos23 as a practical alternative to controlling the full density of the state distribution \cite{Chen21, Moyalan21 .}}
elements.sent-2411-199: Robotics applications of CS include path planning \cite{Okamoto19, Knaup23a , manipulation \cite{Lee22 , soft landing \cite{Ridderhof18 and multi-agent control \cite{Saravanos21, Saravanos22 to name only a few.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-200: Such modifications are particularly relevant to safety critical systems, where the system entering certain states can lead to permanent damage.
elements.sent-2411-201: Nevertheless, the inclusion of nonlinear dynamics and chance constraints complicate CS problems since they require conservative approximations to produce a convex subproblem during each iteration of the algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-202: In fact, these approximations might limit the ability of such approaches to find high quality --- or even feasible --- solutions as the amount of constraints increases.
elements.sent-2411-203: Current literature avoids this problem by only considering linear state constraints that produce a convex polytope of feasible states \cite{Okamoto19, Knaup23a, Pilipovsky21 .}
elements.sent-2411-204: Nonconvex state spaces are then managed by decomposing them into convex polytopes, then employing more expensive mixed-integer programming methods to find trajectories across polytope boundaries \cite{Okamoto19 .}
elements.sent-2411-205: In contrast, this paper presents an algorithm for effectively controlling systems with nonlinear dynamics and nonconvex state constraints.
elements.sent-2411-206: Due to our method's ability to tolerate infeasibility in iterates prior to convergence, it is able to handle nonconvex state constraints directly without decomposing the state space into convex regions.
elements.sent-2411-207: In contrast to other methods for nonlinear CS problems, our algorithm relies on an operator splitting method.
elements.sent-2411-208: Operator splitting is a numerical technique where a function can be decomposed into two or more subproblems and a specialized method is used to tackle each subproblem in an alternating manner.
elements.sent-2411-209: While originally used for solving differential equations, it is also applicable to optimization problems.
elements.sent-2411-210: Operator splitting approaches using the ADMM are becoming increasingly popular methods to solve optimal control problems due to their ability to decouple complex problems into simpler subproblems that can be solved in parallel \cite{Sindhwani17, Saravanos23b .}
elements.sent-2411-211: Several successful applications can be found in trajectory optimization \cite{Sindhwani17, Wang21 , model predictive control \cite{Rey20 , multi-agent control \cite{Saravanos23b, Pereira22, Chen2021b, Ferranti22, Schwager22 , manipulation \cite{Aydinoglu22 , legged locomotion \cite{Zhou20, Budhiraja19 , etc..}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-212: Finally, we define the proximal operator: \begin{equation}{\operatorname{prox}}_{\rho, f}(z) = {\operatorname{argmin}_x} f(x) + \frac{\rho}{2} \| x - z\|_2^2.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-213: The initial and terminal conditions are given by: \begin{equation}\func{E}[X_0] = \mu_{ic}, \func{C}[X_0] = \Sigma_{ic}, \nonumber \func{E}[X_{t_f}] = \mu_{tc}, \func{C}[X_{t_f}] = \Sigma_{tc}. \label{eq:boundary conditions}\end{equation} The state is subject to $N$ nonlinear constraints, (e.g., workspace obstacles) defined by functions $h_i(x)$.
elements.sent-2411-214: Each such function $h_i(x)$ defines a set $\set{H _i \define \{x\ |\ h_i(x) \leq 0\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-215: Since the support of $X_t$ is not compact, it is impossible to satisfy these constraints almost surely.
elements.sent-2411-216: This requirement is relaxed to only hold with high probability via the chance constraint: \begin{equation}\func{P}\{X_t \in \set{H}\} \leq \delta, \set{H} \define \bigcup_{i = 1}^N \set{H}_i. \label{eq:chance constraint}\end{equation} Finally, the controls are selected to minimize in expectation a function $c(x)$ plus a quadratic control defined by $R \in \set{S ^n_{++}$ tallied at each time step $t$.}
elements.sent-2411-217: Therefore, the complete covariance steering problem is formulated as: \footnote{ The terminal covariance constraint can also be relaxed to an inequality constraint, i.e., $\func{C [X_{t_f ] \preceq \Sigma_{\mathrm{tc }$, which indicates that the terminal covariance of the system simply needs to be ``smaller'' than $\Sigma_{\mathrm{tc }$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-218: Specifically, for a state $X_t$, it holds that: \begin{equation}\func{P}\qty{X_t \in \set{H}} \leq \sum_{i = 1}^N \func{P}\qty{h_i(X_t) \leq 0}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-219: Specifically, each nonlinear constraint is formulated as a half-plane constraint given by the linearization of $h_i(x)$, i.e., \begin{equation}\bar{h}_{i, t}(x) \define \transp{a}_{i, t} x + b_{i, t} \leq 0,\end{equation} where $a_{i, t \define \partial_x h_i(\bar{x _{t ), b_{i, t \define h(\bar{x _t) - \transp{a _{i, t}\bar{x _t$ Since $X_t$ is Gaussian, each hyperplane constraint can be formulated as a deterministic function of $(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$, \begin{equation}\func{P}\qty{\transp{a}_{i, t} X_t + b_{i, t} \leq 0} \leq \delta' \iff g_{i, t}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t) \leq 0,\end{equation} where: \begin{equation}g_{i, t}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t) \define \transp{a}_{i, t} \mu_t + b_{i, t} + \inv{\Phi}\qty(1 - \delta')\sqrt{\transp{a}_{i, t}\Sigma_t a_{i, t}} \leq 0.\nonumber\end{equation} Here, $\inv{\Phi (\cdot)$ is the inverse of the Gaussian cumulative density function (CDF) and $\delta' \define \nicefrac{\delta {N}$.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-220: However, since it is a concave function, a linearization of $g_i(\mu, \Sigma)$ will provide a conservative approximation.
elements.sent-2411-221: In this section, we explain how the ADMM is used to formulate an operator splitting scheme that solves the local covariance steering problem \cref{eq:lq cov steer prob .}
elements.sent-2411-222: Nevertheless, even a single state chance constraint produces a coupling resulting in a sizeable SDP that cannot be parallelized \cite{Balci24 .}
elements.sent-2411-223: Our algorithm is a two-level optimization scheme, where the outer level corresponds to a trust-region successive convexification approach, while the inner level involves solving convex SDPs.
elements.sent-2411-224: The convergence of the inner problems \eqref{eq:admm cov steer prob to their optimal solution is straightforward to establish using established results about the convergence of ADMM due to the convexity of the cost terms and the full-rank structure of the linear equality (identity) constraint matrices \cite{Deng16 .}}
elements.sent-2411-225: Given the guaranteed convergence of the inner convex problems, we can then employ a convergence analysis for trust-region sequential convexification methods (e.g. \cite{Mao16 ) to prove the convergence of the outer level, and thus the overall algorithm to a stationary point of the original non-convex optimization problem \eqref{eq:nonlinear cov steer prob .}}
elements.sent-2411-226: A formal exposition of this convergence analysis will be illustrated in an extended version of this manuscript.
elements.sent-2411-227: Since our algorithm allows for temporary infeasibility during intermediate steps, it is important to properly establish a stopping criteria.
elements.sent-2411-228: In the case of the inner-level problem, the primal residual of the ADMM problem can be used as the stopping criterion.
elements.sent-2411-229: The problem is considered to have fully converged when the residuals are below a predetermined tolerance.
elements.sent-2411-230: In a similar manner, for the outer-level problem, we can use the trust region residuals as a stopping criteria.
elements.sent-2411-231: In practice, we observed that a fixed number of inner-level iterations and using constraint satisfaction as a criterion to terminate the outer problem iterations yields safe controls.
elements.sent-2411-232: However, to satisfy the convergence guarantees, the inner and outer problems must both run until they fully converge.
elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{Covariance Steering via Operator Splitting} \label{alg:csos} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require Initial trajectory $\tau^{(0)} \define (\bar{x}^{(0)}_{0:t_f}, \bar{u}_{0:t_f}^{(0)}, \bar{\Sigma}_{0:t_f}^{(0)})$. \Repeat \State $A_{0:t_f}^{(k)}, B_{0:t_f}^{(k)}, d_{0:t_f}^{(k)}, Q_{0:t_f}^{(k)}, q_{0:t_f}^{(k)}, c_{i, 0:t_f}^{(k)}, w_{i, 0:t_f}^{(k)}, G_{i, 0:t_f}^{(k)}$ $\gets$ TaylorExpansions$(\tau^{(k)})$. \State $\mu_{0:t_f}^{(k)}, \Sigma_{0:t_f}^{(k)}, v_{0:t_f}^{(k)}, K_{0:t_f}^{(k)} \gets$ Solve with Alg. \ref{alg:os}. \State $\tau^{(k + 1)} \gets$ ForwardPass$(\mu_{0:t_f}^{(k)}, \Sigma_{0:t_f}^{(k)}, v_{0:t_f}^{(k)}, K_{0:t_f}^{(k)})$ \Until{Converged} \end{algorithmic}
elements.figure-2411-007: \captionsetup[subfigure]{labelformat=empty} \subfloat[]{ \includegraphics[width=0.65\columnwidth, trim={0cm 0cm 0cm 0cm},clip]{images/linear_sdp2_new.png} \label{fig:linear_sdp} } \subfloat[]{ \includegraphics[width=0.65\columnwidth, trim={0cm 0cm 0cm 0cm},clip]{images/linear_admm3.png} \label{fig:linear_admm} } \subfloat[]{ \includegraphics[width=0.635\columnwidth, trim={0cm 0cm 0cm 0cm},clip]{images/infeasibility_sdp_new.png} \label{fig: issue} } \caption{The results of the ablation study conducted in \cref{ssec:ablation}. Our algorithm found a solution that exhibits a lower cost while also satisfying tighter safety constraints. a) The solution found by the baseline method. Notice that, despite a loser safety requirement, the covariances are much tighter and the trajectory takes larger steps compared to our solution. This is an artifact of the optimizer stuck in a bad local minima where the only feasible solution requires precisely placed means and covariances. b) The solution found by our algorithm. c) This schematic visually depicts why traditional solvers struggle to solve \cref{eq:lq cov steer prob} as part of iterative algorithms. The trajectory $\tau_{\mathrm{orig}}$ is the trajectory around which the approximations occur. The half-planes shown correspond to the linearized chance constraints. The set of points that are feasible with respect to the chance constraint and those that are dynamically feasible cannot both be satisfied.} \label{fig:csetup}
elements.sent-2411-233: In our experiments, to enforce obstacle avoidance we use a signed distance function to each obstacle as the constraint $h_i(X_t)$.
elements.sent-2411-234: All optimization problems are solved with CVXPY \cite{Diamond16 and MOSEK on Intel i9-14900K.. \cite{mosek_python .}}

elements.sent-2411-235: The initial and target covariances are specified through $\Sigma_0=0.1I_3$ and $\Sigma_{t_f \preceq 0.1I_3$, while $D_t=0.01I_3$.}
elements.sent-2411-236: For this experiment, we placed 5 obstacles as shown in \cref{fig:unicycle .}
elements.sent-2411-237: Optimal planning in this environment requires navigating through a somewhat narrow central channel and appropriately reducing the covariance.
elements.sent-2411-238: The covariance is then free to expand to satisfy the terminal constraint when in the open space near the terminal condition.
elements.sent-2411-239: Then, to demonstrate the effectiveness of our algorithm, we also tested it in 10 different environment configurations.
elements.sent-2411-240: We warm-started each environment with an initial solution to aid in faster convergence.
elements.sent-2411-241: While our method can handle poor initializations, the outer optimization process may be slow to converge.
elements.sent-2411-242: In practical applications, a better method to initialize the system is to use a motion planning algorithm, like RRT$^\star$, to find a feasible initial trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-243: The statistics of these experiments averaged over all 10 environments is shown in \cref{tabl:results .}
elements.sent-2411-244: The solution for each environment was obtained by running \cref{alg:csos for 10 iterations and solving \cref{eq:lq cov steer prob using 15 ADMM iterations.}}
elements.sent-2411-245: On average, our method required 2.67 seconds for computation.

elements.sent-2411-251: This improvement is due to the fact that the algorithm is tolerant to infeasible iterates during the optimization feature that often appear due to the sensitivity and conservatism of the necessary approximations.
elements.sent-2411-252: The advantages of our method were demonstrated on a variety of tasks including both simulation and hardware experiments.
elements.sent-2411-253: There is a variety of possible extensions and applications for the proposed distribution steering methodology.
elements.sent-2411-254: One promising direction would be to replace the standard ADMM scheme with other variants that are intended for handling probability distributions such as Bregman ADMM \cite{Wang14 .}
elements.sent-2411-255: Combining our algorithm with sampling-based motion planning \cite{Lavalle06 for finding safe plans, similar in spirit to LQR-Trees \cite{Tedrake10 , could also be an interesting area of research.}}
elements.sent-2411-256: Finally, we plan to explore integrating the proposed approach into learning-to-optimize frameworks for operator splitting methods \cite{Sambharya24, Saravanos25 to further enhance the computational efficiency and scalability of our algorithm.}
